title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Rescue students take part in earthquake simulation DOWNTOWN: Class had to save all victims'in 12-story building. By Jeff St. John Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 29, 2001) Cory Aist and Bean of the Alaska Search and rescue Dogs move on to find other victims during an LTR training Systems exercise at the McKay building. Kristina Compelube plays an accident victim with leg injuries. A dozen students took part in the drill. (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) A dozen students armed with rescue gear got as close as they ever want to get to a true disaster at the vacant MacKay building in downtown Anchorage on Friday. The simulated one seemed real enough: An earthquake had struck, lights were out, people were hurt, bleeding and screaming. What are you going to do? Maerene Tay…

title: Tourism industry told it's vulnerable
summary: Alaskans are no safer than New Yorkers or anyone else when its comes to terrorist threats, and tourism businesses are particularly vulnerable, said terrorism expert Peter Tarlow in Anchorage on Wednesday. The leisure and travel industry has been asleep as far as terrorism is concerned, and despite last month's attacks on the World Trade Center and Pentagon, security remains far too lax, said Tarlow, in remarks to the Alaska Travel Industry Association convention at the Egan Center. The cruise ship industry is a "sitting duck," said Tarlow, a sociologist and rabbi at Texas A&M University. Tarlow trains the FBI and police chiefs around the country on how to combat terrorism, with a focus on tourism. On his journey this week from Texas to Alaska, airport security guards failed to ask him abou…

title: Judge's decision on Cook Inlet belugas appealed
summary: Arguing that Cook Inlet's troubled beluga whales need more protection to ensure that they don't go extinct, a coalition of environmental groups and an Anchorage-based Native hunter have appealed a federal court decision in their legal struggle to get the local whales listed under the Endangered Species Act. Last summer, U.S. District Judge James Robertson of Washington, D.C., ruled that the National Marine Fisheries Service acted within its authority when it listed the local belugas as depleted instead of endangered or threatened. "We felt like the judge had done a very shallow analysis of our case and the brief we submitted," said Kris Balliet, Alaska director for the Ocean Conservancy. "There were questions left unanswered and issues not dealt with." The "depleted" designation under the …

title: Botelho: Subsistence solution close
summary: Juneau -- A final solution on solving the state's subsistence dilemma could include changes to both state and federal law, said the head of a panel appointed by the governor to resolve the issue. Attorney General Bruce Botelho said Wednesday he was optimistic the group could craft something that would finally pass muster with a sharply divided state Legislature. "I think where we're headed has the potential of enlisting the requisite support of the Legislature," said Botelho, chairman of the panel. The 11-member panel appointed by Gov. Tony Knowles is expected to complete a draft of a state constitutional amendment today to lay the groundwork for that plan. The group is trying to resolve a conflict between federal law that establishes a rural priority for subsistence hunting and fishing an…

title: Labels worry principals
summary: If reaction from a roomful of principals is a sign of what's to come, Alaska educators will take this summer's labeling of their schools very personally. About 50 principals gathered Tuesday in a small meeting room in the Sheraton Anchorage Hotel as part of the 35th Annual Fall Principal's Conference. They learned about Alaska School Designators, a state system that will annually label every Alaska public school distinguished, successful, deficient or in-crisis, beginning in August 2002. The system was signed into law in June 1998 with Senate Bill 36. The principals Tuesday learned these labels will likely be based entirely on student test scores. Their reactions ranged from anger to hopelessness -- one elementary principal even wept -- as Mark Leal, the Department of Education assessment …

title: State backs ban on baiting eagles to attract tourists to preserve
summary: Haines -- A state agency has upheld an order banning a local tour operator from baiting eagles inside the Alaska Chilkat Bald Eagle Preserve. In so doing, Alaska Department of Natural Resources Commissioner Pat Pourchot emphasized the state's position that commercial tour operators aren't necessarily guaranteed traditional use rights to do business in the preserve. Residents in Haines and Klukwan -- a Tlingit Indian village surrounded by the preserve -- were guaranteed traditional use rights to hunt, trap, fish, harvest firewood and use motorized vehicles when the preserve was founded in l982. The traditional use rights were contingent on the activity not harming eagles or their habitat. Duck and Karen Hess, who operate River Adventures, a popular jetboat tour on the Chilkat River near Klu…

summary: In Alaska News Anglers protest king limit change By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 29, 2001) Homer -- Complaining they were blindsided by a new rule that could shut down winter fishing for king salmon, Kachemak Bay anglers gathered here this week to plan a way to save their popular saltwater fishery. Ron Berglund holds the fish his father, Dennis, caught during the 5th Annual Homer Chamber of Commerce Winter King Salmon Tournament in 1998. The Fish Board adopted a rule earlier this month that would count any winter-caught kings toward an angler's five-king-per-year limit. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Daily News file photo) Nearly 80 fishermen crowded into a local classroom Tuesday night to help the local fish and game advisory committee draft a letter to the state Board o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community datebook (Published: January 28, 2002) SCHOOL LUNCH MONDAY Anchorage: Elementary -- pepperoni pizza or Belgian waffle with sausage links or corn dog, orange smiles, celery sticks, milk. Middle school -- pizza with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, milk. Mat-Su: beef and bean burrito, chilled canned fruit, carrot sticks with dip, milk (white or chocolate). TUESDAY Anchorage: Elementary -- mini corn dogs with potato wedges or Beef Fingers with potato wedges or chicken burrito, sliced apples, carrot sticks, milk. Middle school -- nachos with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, milk. Mat-Su: French toast sticks with syrup, sausage links, chilled canned fruit, juice cup, milk (white or chocolate). MONDAY COMMUNITY Anchorage Pioneers' Hom…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Coast Guard aids 2 boats COD FISHERY: Ice, rough seas force Miss Leona to head south By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 26, 2002) Two fishing vessels that reported trouble Thursday in the icy North Pacific Ocean received assistance Friday from the U.S. Coast Guard and reported no more incidents. The 86-foot Miss Leona and the 75-foot Windjammer encountered severe weather while en route Thursday from Washington state to Sand Point for the upcoming Pacific cod fishery. Rough seas and ice caused several problems on Miss Leona, including a broken pilothouse windshield and long-range radio transmitter. Thursday night, the ships' crews were afraid they might capsize, according to the Coast Guard. Coast Guard cutters Mellon and Storis arrived Friday afternoon at the scen…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska digest (Published: January 26, 2002) WASILLA Teen charged with shooting elderly woman in late-night confrontation An 18-year-old man, apparently distraught over losing his girlfriend, was charged with attempted murder Friday after shooting an elderly Wasilla area woman in the face, Wasilla police said. Andrew Lee Coffman is accused of shooting Mary Rogers after breaking into her home near Mile 3.5 of Knik-Goosebay Road. Rogers, 75, who suffered a broken jaw, was listed in critical condition at Providence Alaska Medical Center in Anchorage. According to police, Coffman told officers he went to the home about 2 a.m. to confront his ex-girlfriend. The girlfriend was living at the home with Rogers' grandson, Joseph Woods, Lt. Craig Robinson said. Woods confronted Coffman in the ya…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Sitka police under investigation ACCUSATIONS: Troopers looking into possible criminal misconduct. The Associated Press (Published: January 26, 2002) Sitka -- Alaska State Troopers were set to begin an investigation Friday of possible criminal misconduct in the Sitka Police Department, according to city officials. The move came a day after Sitka's police chief was put on administrative leave until his resignation becomes effective next month. City Administrator Tony Zimmer told the Sitka Sentinel he put Police Chief Bill McLendon on paid administrative leave "to facilitate an entirely independent investigation." McLendon's leave extends until the effective date of his resignation, Feb. 22. McLendon resigned Jan. 16, citing personal reasons for his departure. His resignation came the s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Village police chief quits over pot claim ALAKANUK: Would-be trooper accused of smoking marijuana with a minor. By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 25, 2002) Felix Edmund's dream of becoming a certified law enforcement officer might have just gone up in smoke. A state trooper cited the 22-year-old Alakanuk village police chief on Jan. 15 for smoking marijuana with a 15-year-old boy, troopers reported Thursday. He submitted his resignation three days later, a city clerk said. In August, Edmund spoke with Anchorage police detective Sean Purcell, who visited the Yukon Delta village of 652 as part of Chief Walt Monegan's publicized outreach to recruit Alaska Natives. Edmund said law enforcement interested him since childhood and his main goal was to become a state troo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska digest (Published: January 25, 2002) ANCHORAGE Democrat longshoreman files intent to run for lieutenant governor Anchorage longshoreman and all-Alaska gas line backer Scott Heyworth announced Thursday that he has filed a letter of intent to run for lieutenant governor. Heyworth is a Democrat and apparently the first in his party to enter the race. "I'm honest, I have a passion for Alaska, and my first concern is Alaskans," Heyworth said in a telephone interview Thursday. Heyworth is a sponsor of an initiative petition that would create an Alaska Natural Gas Development Authority to acquire North Slope natural gas and build a pipeline from Prudhoe Bay to tidewater in Prince William Sound. He's also a member of the municipal Public Safety Commission. Heyworth said he doesn't wan…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Art lover turns passion into crime at Fairbanks gallery RECEPTIONS: Three pieces have been stolen during recent openings. The Associated Press (Published: January 21, 2002) Fairbanks -- A pilfering patron of the arts has been making opening nights at the Well Street Art Co. expensive events for gallery owner and artist David Mollett. In the past four months, three pieces have been stolen during Friday night art openings. The most recent and most expensive item lifted, a Fred Machetanz lithograph print titled "The Hunt" and valued at $3,400, was filched within the first half-hour of an opening reception Jan. 11. In December, a Rik Seeganna soapstone carving, weighing 6 to 8 pounds with a $350 retail value, was stolen right off the gallery counter. "I had just bought it from Rik a coup…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Barrow residents get ready for an hour and 15 minutes of sun today 66 DAYS: Northern community has not seen daylight since Nov. 18. By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 23, 2002) They'll be singing "Here Comes the Sun" in Barrow today. After almost 66 days of night, the big yellow disc will top the horizon at 1:02 p.m. for the first time since it set at 1:41 p.m. Nov. 18. To honor the occasion, tacos at Pepe's North of the Border restaurant, normally $3-$3.50, will sell for $1.02 between noon and 2 p.m. Children at the Ipalook Elementary School will draw suns and write about the sunrise, and 3- and 4-year-olds will have a Welcome Back to the Sun luau, principal Glenn Olson said. Altogether, the sun will make a short, low transit of an hour and 15 minutes, setting a…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Judge cautions state, Tetlow STOP: Jonathan Link urges litigants to end court fight. By Sheila Toomey Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 22, 2002) The judge in the Wally Tetlow case has made one last effort to persuade the combatants to end their fight. This time he's invoked the equilibrium theorem developed by Nobel laureate John Nash, whose life is the subject of the movie "A Beautiful Mind." State prosecutors responded to Judge Jonathan Link's earlier plea by digging in their heels and refusing to consider a settlement. On Dec. 19, after listening to 12 days of testimony, Link had questioned whether resources were being wasted on a dispute over a single-car crash with minor injury to a passenger who has said he wasn't hurt and has no complaint against the driver. Link liken…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaskans seek rule change for foreign cargo carriers EXEMPTION: State may draw more international flights. The Associated Press Wire (Published: January 22, 2002) Fairbanks -- U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens last year unsuccessfully sought to allow foreign air cargo planes landing in Alaska to move their foreign goods to other aircraft and air carriers bound for U.S. destinations. Such transfers are banned under some interpretations of federal law, but a review is coming this year. Stevens, at the behest of state officials, put the exemption in the Senate version of the annual spending bill for the U.S. Department of Transportation. The Senate approved the bill Aug. 1 but the idea was shot down in a conference committee that merged the House and Senate versions in late November. Instead, the c…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Airport ill-prepared for major accident RESCUE Airplane near-disaster focuses attention on problems. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 27, 2002) A China Airlines airplane that narrowly avoided disaster early Friday in Anchorage handed the latest reality check to rescuers who say they're ill-prepared to save mass victims of a plane crash in Cook Inlet. Federal and state officials say the incident at Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport highlights the need for a large-scale rescue plan for the Inlet to prepare for the time when a pilot isn't so lucky. "This thing that happened early (Friday) morning just reinforced that, in the most dramatic way short of killing someone," said Lt. Chuck Lamica, the Alaska State Troopers' statewide search-and-rescue coordi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: January 25, 2002) South Naknek plant to stay closed NAKNEK -- Trident Seafoods won't open its plant in South Naknek this summer, the company announced Thursday. Current market conditions, including low prices and a glut of farmed salmon, are the reasons for the decision, said Vic Scheibert, the company's operations manager for Bristol Bay. The plant usually employs between 150 and 200 people at South Naknek. The company won't buy or process fish at the plant, but it will continue to provide boat storage, vessel tie-up, groceries and gear sales to fishermen. Trident's operations at its North Naknek plant will not be affected by the closure, the company said. Retirement community to expand Mountain Rose Estates in Palmer announced it will add 34 house…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Snowmachine rider helps out dogs Q&A: Snowmachine driver has the record for breaking trail for dogs (Published: January 20, 2002) Scott Duby says he is looking forward to breaking the Iditarod Trail for mushers for a few more years. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Scott Duby Age: 47 Occupation/profession: Heavy-equipment operator in the summer; owner and operator of Alaska Snowmobile Salvage in Anchorage, which buys, sells and repairs snowmachines. Q. What's your great outdoor accomplishment? A. Duby has been on the snowmachine team that breaks trail for the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race since 1989, longer than any other trail breaker. Duby and his cousin, Kirk Duby, have led trail breaking crews for more than a decade. A friend, Rick Koch, now president of the Iditarod…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaska Digest (Published: January 24, 2002) KODIAK Police charge alleged shooter with first-degree attempted murder Kodiak police arrested a 50-year-old man Tuesday afternoon and charged him with first-degree attempted murder. Domi Ganotisi of Kodiak fired a weapon at another man in a residential section of the city, police said. Police arrested Ganotisi after responding to a disturbance involving two men, including one who was armed. The other man was not injured. Police said investigators are continuing to interview people connected to incident. -- The Associated Press NOME Women accused of trying to sneak liquor to Gambell Alaska State Troopers in Nome confiscated 12 bottles of alcohol from an 18-year-old Shishmaref woman, troopers said Wednesday. Troopers arrested Gertrude "Gerti…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Tribal ire Natives fear Indian Affairs plan would hurt villages By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 25, 2002) Alaska's tribes joined others across the country this week in opposing a Bush administration proposal to reorganize the Bureau of Indian Affairs. The reorganization was proposed last November by Interior Secretary Gale Norton as a way for the government to gain better control of trust accounts mismanaged in the past by the BIA. The government has been sued by Indians claiming as much as $10 billion in royalties owed to individual Indians was lost because of faulty bookkeeping and theft. But opponents of the reorganization plan, which would remove trust operations from the BIA, say it could lead to dissolving the main federal agency dealing with Native Ameri…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | In Brief (Published: January 21, 2002) ANCHORAGE Police hunt art thieves, two purse-snatchers Police are searching for two thieves who are targeting downtown galleries and gift shops. Working as a team, the pair will enter a business. While one distracts the salesperson, the other slips valuable carvings into his jacket and runs from the store. Between Jan. 7 and 10, the two stole carvings totaling about $10,000, including a whale bone carving of a bear with an ivory fish in its mouth, a soapstone carving of a bear holding a fish carved from antler, and a sheep horn with a Dall sheep carved inside. Both thieves appear to be white. One is 5 feet, 4 inches to 5 feet, 8 inches tall, has sandy hair and is clean-shaven with a thin build. The other is about 5 feet, 11 inches with a black g…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Don't drink the water in Sterling GASOLINE Fuel tank leak underground leads to testing, precautions. The Associated Press (Published: January 26, 2002) Kenai -- A gasoline leak from a subsurface storage tank at a former Sterling gas station is raising concerns about the safety of drinking water in nearby wells, including one at an elementary school. The contamination at the former Sterling Zip Mart, which closed last year, was discovered during environmental work by a company hired by the station's owners, Whittier Properties Inc. Tests of the integrity of the underground tanks conducted two years ago showed no indication of a spill at that time, according to the Department of Environmental Conservation. However, Paul Horwath, an environmental specialist with the DEC's Soldotna offic…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Man is killed when stolen vehicle flips on DeBarr ACCIDENT: Driver dies at scene, but passenger survives crash. By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 9, 2001) A 22-year-old man died early Monday when the stolen vehicle he was driving flipped and rolled on DeBarr Road into a gas station parking lot, Anchorage police said. Employees of the Williams Express at 5501 DeBarr Road called police at 4:19 a.m. when the green Geo Tracker rolled into their parking lot. Officers found the driver dead. His name has not been released pending notification of his family. Passenger Frank Charley, 21, was taken to Alaska Regional Hospital, where he was treated for head injuries and released. The Tracker was reported stolen Saturday from a home in the city's west side, said police spoke…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Agency seeks funds to cover increased oil field activity $500,000: Jump in leasing, production requires more staff, director says. By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published: January 18, 2002) Juneau -- Oil production is up, state officials say, but success comes with a price. Mark Myers, director of the Division of Oil and Gas, told a legislative committee Thursday he needs $500,000 more next year, partly to hire staff to handle an increased workload. Department of Natural Resources Commissioner Pat Pourchot told the House Special Committee on Oil and Gas that in the fiscal year that ended in June 2001, oil production rose for the first time in at least 10 years. The increase was slight -- probably 30,000-40,000 barrels a day -- but it was good news after years of watching produ…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Visitors bureau reports record 2001 :Marketing organization says 2001 a banner year for city. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 18, 2002) Despite a sharp drop in tourism after the terrorist attacks in September, the Anchorage Convention & Visitors Bureau enjoyed a record year in 2001. The city's main marketing organization sold $78 million worth of conventions, meetings and events, said Bruce Bustamante, president and chief executive. That's a 10 percent increase over the prior year and about $1 million more than anticipated, he said. "We secured business as far out as 2006," Bustamante said prior to a Thursday speech at the Egan Center on the bureau's year. Bustamante also estimates Anchorage generated at least $86.5 million in tourism sales and services. That…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: BLOOD BANK The Blood Bank of Alaska will be closed today, with regular hours resuming Wednesday. Hours are 10 a.m.-6 p.m. Monday through Saturday at 4000 Laurel St. Appointments are recommended at 563-3110 in Anchorage, 1-907-260-5672 in Soldotna and 1-907-376-1195 in Wasilla. Needs include: O-positive, 122 donors O-negative, 34 donors A-positive, 77 donors A-negative, 34 donors B-positive, 50 donors B-negative, 5 donors AB-positive, 4 donors AB-negative, 2 donors TODAY COMMUNITY Alaskan Sled Dog and Racing Association's race series features a juniors race, 11 a.m. at the Tozier Track, south side of Tudor Road east of Lake Otis Parkway. (562-2235) Alaskan Prospectors Society regular meeting is canceled. (248-1060) WEDNESDAY COMMUNITY Anchorage Genealogical Society 7 p.m., Loussac Library, …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Naknek lifeguard charged with indecent viewing POOL: Wireless video camera taped girls changing. By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 18, 2002) A 58-year-old Naknek man used a wireless video camera to record young girls changing clothes at the village's swimming pool, Bristol Bay Borough officials said Thursday. John Knutsen, a volunteer lifeguard, is charged with indecent viewing. He was detained Monday and released Tuesday on $2,000 bail. A pool employee spotted the video camera Saturday, according to a police news release. George Castaneda, borough manager, said the camera appears to have been discovered the same day it was installed. The camera, which Castaneda described as pencil-sized, was "just laying there in plain sight on top of a towel dispenser" in the g…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Vetoes overridden in new session BILLS: Measure requiring governor to wait five days to replace U.S. senator passes. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: January 17, 2002) Juneau -- Lawmakers in the Republican-dominated Legislature on Wednesday passed four bills vetoed by Democratic Gov. Tony Knowles last year. In a joint House-Senate session, voting largely along party lines, lawmakers overrode Knowles vetoes on measures on campaign contributions, replacement of a U.S. senator and the transfer of 3,500 acres to the Denali Borough. Senate Bill 166 would require the governor to wait five days before filling the vacancy of a U.S. senator. Democrats complained that the measure was a partisan tactic that would allow U.S. Sen. Frank Murkowski, who is running for governor, to …

title: Downtown ride leads to sexual assault
summary: An 18-year-old woman was raped Saturday night by a man who gave her a ride as she walked along a downtown street, Anchorage police said Monday. The woman was walking to the Five Fifteen Club, 515 W. Fourth Avenue, about 9 p.m. to find a friend to give her a ride home when a van pulled up near Fourth Avenue and Cordova Street, police spokeswoman Anita Shell said. The driver asked her for a lighter and then offered her a ride. After the woman accepted, they picked up a bottle of liquor and drove around town drinking, Shell said. When the woman began to feel the affects of the alcohol, she went to the back of the van to lie down. The man then followed her into the back and sexually assaulted her, Shell said. After the assault, the woman left the van and ran to the Applebees at 4331 Credit Uni…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community Profile (Published: January 24, 2002) SAVOONGA POPULATION: 643 (December 2001) LOCATION: On the northern coast of St. Lawrence Island in the Bering Sea, 164 miles west of Nome and 39 miles southeast of Gambell. DESCRIPTION: A traditional Yup'ik Eskimo village with a subsistence lifestyle and economy surrounding walrus and whale hunting; it is hailed as the "Walrus Capital of the World." HISTORY: St. Lawrence Island has been inhabited intermittently for the past 2,000 years by both Alaskan and Siberian Yup'ik Eskimos. The island had numerous villages totaling 4,000 people by the 19th century. The city was incorporated in 1969. Gambell and Savoonga did not participate in the 1971 Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act and instead opted for title to the 1.136 million acres in the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Judge erred in taking gun permit, court says STIR: Mental illness, right to carry are at heart of Wagner case. By Mary Pemberton The Associated Press (Published: January 6, 2002) Judge Natalie Finn didn't hesitate to take away Timothy Wagner's permit to carry a concealed handgun after hearing he believes there's a computer chip implanted in his head and he's been injected with deadly chemicals. But a state appeals court ruled recently that Judge Finn erred. The court declared that under Alaska law, mental illness alone cannot be considered in deciding fitness to carry a concealed weapon. And state officials say they are trying to figure out how they can turn Wagner down if he asks for his permit to be returned. The case has caused a stir among people who say it demonstrates excessive…

title: Nome youth home director ousted
summary: The acting director of a Nome youth home was dismissed Wednesday after a week on the job and five days after the state removed the home's teenage residents. The Nome Receiving Home's board of directors unanimously decided to terminate Theresa Rismeyer days after she announced a series of new policies that rankled staffers, according to Norma Niclas, board president. "Theresa had some very good ideas," Niclas said. "But it was like too much, too soon." On Dec. 28, about eight disgruntled staffers angrily confronted Rismeyer in a scene witnessed by at least two teens from the home. Rismeyer described the confrontation as involving screaming and finger-pointing. On the same day, an 18-year-old client complained to a state probation officer that a staffer had cursed at him, Niclas said. The pr…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Video cameras capture DWIs DONATIONS: MADD wants to ensure drunken drivers are convicted. By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 14, 2002) Anchorage police officer Jim Bucher uses the video camera and LCD display, top right, purchased by Mothers Against Drunk Driving. "It documents everything, from my observations to what the person is saying to me to how they act," he said. The funds to buy the camera came from the $35 fee that convicted drunken drivers pay MADD to participate in a victim impact panel. (Photo by Evan R. Steinhauser / Anchorage Daily News) Anchorage police arrested 40 percent more drunken drivers during the recent holiday season than they did two years ago, and Mothers Against Drunk Driving is working to get more police car video cameras to make sure …

title: Snowmachiner dies in avalanche
summary: A 35-year-old Anchorage man died in an avalanche Sunday afternoon while snowmachining near Hope. The slide occurred in the Swetmann Mine area of Chugach National Forest, according to Alaska State Troopers. The area is at the end of Palmer Creek Road, about a dozen miles south of Hope. Russel Foster was in a group of seven snowmachiners, troopers spokesman Greg Wilkinson said Sunday night, and that group encountered five others. Foster and another snowmachiner had broken away from the other 10 when the snow slid toward them. "The survivor went to where the other members of the group were. Then they went back to where the avalanche had occurred," he said. Foster "did have a beacon on, and other members were able to locate his body very quickly. In fact, they estimated they were able to get b…

title: Ideal vacation memory?
summary: Passengers of an Alaska Sightseeing tour bus help one another up a slope of the Glenn Highway on Tuesday near the north Eagle River exit. The bus, carrying 38 people, was evacuated after its engine caught fire while traveling to Talkeetna. Bus driver Lori Stebbins quickly extinguished the fire and no one was hurt. A second bus picked up the passengers and continued north toward Denali National Park.

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Restoring the flow Efforts focus on the mouth of Ship Creek By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 14, 2002) Ducks paddle downstream of the dam. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Ship Creek likely won't be transformed into a free-flowing urban stream anytime soon, but people hoping to restore it to its natural condition are chipping away at obstructions that prevent fish from spawning upstream. The leading group behind the effort is the Anchorage Waterways Council, working mainly with the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers. The idea is to someday remove the lower three of the creek's four dams to create 12 miles of undammed river for wild fish runs. Almost no salmon get past th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Game Board to look at Tier II HUNTERS: Panel to consider nine solutions from Alaskans. By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 17, 2002) When the Alaska Board of Game meets Friday, one of the thornier issues its members will consider is the "Tier II" system, a method for determining which Alaskans may hunt when game is limited. The guidelines have long spurred controversy and have come under particular attack in recent years by those excluded from hunting caribou in the Nelchina basin. Located northeast of Anchorage, the basin is popular with urban and local hunters because it's easy to reach from the road system and the region's open tundra is easy to hunt. Because of declines in the Nelchina caribou herd, the state issued 2,000 subsistence caribou permits for …

title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: Alaska pollock fuel seafood export The United States exported a record $104 million worth of seafood to Germany in the first nine months of last year, more than double the seafood exports there for all of 2000, according to the U.S. Foreign Agricultural Service. Because of reduced harvest by European fleets, the amount of Alaska pollock exported was a major reason for the increase, the FAS said. Also, U.S. salmon, lobster and dogfish grew, although the service noted U.S. salmon has only a 4.5 percent market share in Germany, where Norwegian and other farmed salmon dominate. Home loan delinquency rate rises The Alaska Housing Finance Corporation reported the delinquency rate on all loans other than mobile homes in November 2001 was 2.97 percent, up .15 percent from a year earlier. The delin…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Federal court mulls logging injunction TONGASS: Timber harvest was halted as a result of judge's order. The Associated Press (Published: January 3, 2002) Juneau -- A federal court hearing today will discuss whether an injunction against logging is needed in the Tongass National Forest in Southeast Alaska. The hearing in U.S. District Court in Anchorage comes after a decision by the U.S. Forest Service last year to halt logging in the national forest following a ruling by Judge James Singleton, the same judge who will preside over oral arguments between attorneys representing environmental groups, the Forest Service, the timber industry and Southeast Alaska communities. In a March court order, Singleton stopped the Forest Service from altering the wilderness character of roadless area…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: KENAI POPULATION: 6,942 (December 2001) LOCATION: On the western coast of the Kenai Peninsula, on the Kenai Spur Highway, about 65 air miles and 155 highway miles southwest of Anchorage. DESCRIPTION: The largest city on the Kenai Peninsula and the center of the Cook Inlet oil and gas industry. Other important economic sectors include tourism, fishing and lumber. About one of every eight residents is at least partly Alaska Native. HISTORY: Before Russian settlement, Kenai was a Dena'ina Athabaskan Indian village. The first dirt road from Anchorage was constructed in 1951. In 1957, oil was discovered at Swanson River -- the first major Alaska oil strike. The city was incorporated in 1960. Source: Alaska Dept. of Community and Economic Development www.dced.state.ak.us/mra/CF_CIS.cfm

title: High winds wrench roofing off Hillside home
summary: Powerful wind gusts blew off one-fourth of a Hillside home's roof Tuesday morning. Guilford Prickette was in his home, 9121 Brewsters Drive, getting his children ready to leave for the day. At about 8:45 a.m. Prickette was standing in the master bedroom when he heard a loud crashing noise. He grabbed his boots and jacket and ran outside. "I saw part of my roof was missing," Prickette said. When he left the house mid-morning, the metal sheeting was flipped over onto the other side of the roof. "The plywood had gone 40 to 50 feet down the hill," he said. Wood provided some cover in the damaged area, Prickette said. The Anchorage Fire Department received a call about the roof in the afternoon. Spokesman Tom Kempton said that when firefighters arrived at about 4 p.m. the section of roof had pe…

title: Historic Oscar Gill House has watched the city grow
summary: The Oscar Gill House on West 10th Avenue is one of two famous old "Oscar" buildings in Anchorage; the other is the Oscar Anderson House. Both tell of the earliest years of the city when Anchorage was a crude little pioneer town whose residents nevertheless aspired to gracious living. The Gill house was built by and named for a man who did just about everything an enterprising person could do in the Upper Cook Inlet area in the first half of the 20th century. Between his arrival in Alaska in 1907 and his death in 1947, Gill ran a sawmill, delivered the mail, prospected for gold, opened one of the city's first auto service stations, served on the City Council and as Anchorage mayor and was speaker of the territorial House of Representatives. Gill's two-story frame house has experienced numer…

title: Bunde warms redistricting hot seat
summary: The people who called Rep. Con Bunde to testify in the redistricting trial Thursday put him in a tough spot. Bunde, R-Anchorage, was called to trash some of the new district lines, specifically to explain how impossible it would be for one legislator to fairly represent a House district that includes both South Anchorage and Valdez. He did so. The two cities have little in common and are often on opposite sides of issues, Bunde said. He has voluntarily visited Valdez just once in 40 years and four or five times on legislative business. He would try to be fair but would find it difficult to promote Valdez interests when they conflict with Anchorage's, he said, especially since Valdez would have only about 30 percent of the voters. Then Bunde acknowledged that he intends to benefit from the …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community profile (Published: December 27, 2001) Tonsina POPULATION: 92 (2000) LOCATION: At mile 79 on the Richardson Highway, south of the Tonsina River, next to Kenny Lake, 52 miles northeast of Valdez. DESCRIPTION: With no clearly defined community, Tonsina is best characterized as an occupied geographic area. About one of every 10 residents is Alaska Native or part Native. Six individuals reported self-employment in the 1990 census, all providing professional services. Roadhouses, the Ernestine State Highway Maintenance camp and Alyeska Pipeline Pump Station 12 are the nearest employers. Subsistence activities supplement income. During the 2000 U.S. census, there were 45 housing units, 11 of them vacant, with two of the vacant units used only seasonally. HISTORY: A U.S. Army Sign…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Battling bias Agency caseload falls but racism claims rise By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 5, 2001) Keith Laws said he lost his job at a construction company because of his race. He took his case to the Alaska Human Rights Commission. Debbie Hughes said she lost her job at a major accounting firm because she was a parent. She took her case to the Alaska Human Rights Commission. The Governor's Commission on Tolerance, formed in response to recent attacks on Alaska Natives, is traveling the state, gathering Alaskans' stories about discrimination in their lives, so it can shape public policy. Meanwhile, the Alaska Human Rights Commission is in its 38th year of doing something very similar, with the goal of preventing and eliminating discrimination. An impartial,…

title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: Local bank reports record earnings First National Bank of Alaska reported record profits last year of $38.8 million, up 24 percent over 2000, said D.H. Cuddy, chairman and president of the Anchorage-based bank. The previous record earnings were $35 million in 1993. Fourth-quarter profits grew 19 percent to $10.9 million, he said. As of Dec. 31, the bank's loan portfolio reached $915 million, up 19 percent from a year earlier. Total assets, including loans, were $1.79 billion, up 7 percent. Deposits grew 8 percent to $1.1 billion, Cuddy said. Real estate brokers merge Two Anchorage commercial real estate brokerage, management and leasing companies have merged. Pacific Tower Properties Inc. and H&R Management Inc. will operate under the Pacific Tower name, said Mark Rowley, president of Paci…

title: Predator control comments were premature
summary: State biologists studying predator-prey relationships around McGrath have not yet settled on a plan to kill wolves and bears, and they won't without first getting the go-ahead from a governor-appointed task force. That was the main point made by the governor's office and the Alaska Department of Fish and Game in a joint press release issued Sunday. The press release, which illustrated how touchy even talking about predator control can be, also rebutted comments made to the media by a state biologist. Patrick Valkenburg, research coordinator for the Division of Wildlife Conservation's Interior Region, told an Associated Press reporter that it may be necessary to kill about 50 black bears and 40-50 wolves and to relocate about a dozen grizzlies to rebuild McGrath's moose population. Rob Bosw…

title: Solution eyed for clogged road
summary: Snug Harbor Road apparently is a little too snug for snowmachiners. Near the tail end of winter, the narrow, unpaved road in Cooper Landing doubles as a staging area for snowmachiners who zip into the Kenai Mountains to cruise through 10- to 20-foot-deep powder. Parked trucks with long trailers jam whatever space is available alongside the road. Drivers seeking parking often have to slowly back their trucks and trailers along the icy road. Some jackknife trying to turn around. Others occasionally slide off the road. "You can envision the nightmare, and that's exactly what we've got," said Dennis Merkes of Sterling, president of an organization called Kenai Peninsula Public Land Users Group, which advocates for more recreational access into parks and forests. The group is developing an agre…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community datebook (Published: January 9, 2002) SCHOOL LUNCH TODAY Anchorage: Elementary -- Breaded chicken nuggets with potato wedges or spaghetti and garlic roll or chicken burrito, celery sticks, Trail Mix with nuts, milk. Middle school -- spaghetti with garlic roll with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, milk. Mat-Su: Honey barbecue dippers, green peas, gelatin with fruit, Tutti-Fruitti Pan Bread, milk (white or strawberry). THURSDAY Anchorage: Elementary -- Bean burrito or roast chicken with mashed potatoes and gravy or corn dog, diced pears, carrot sticks, chocolate chip cookie, milk. Middle school -- deep dish pizza with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, chocolate chip cookie, milk. Mat-Su: Chicken-fried beef strips, potato rounds, fr…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Wireless firm seeks FCC refund PETITION: Alaska Native Wireless loses millions with license in limbo. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 9, 2002) Alaska Native Wireless wants the Federal Communications Commission to refund about $550 million the company paid last year after successfully bidding on wireless licenses now mired in the courts. With the government holding the money interest-free, and with the licenses in limbo, Alaska Native Wireless and 12 other successful bidders are losing $3 million every week in interest payments, according to company representatives and a petition filed with the FCC. The carriers have asked the commission to return a total of $3.1 billion in down payments no later than Jan. 18. Any delay would cause the companies to suffer cont…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Flu cases are rising in Alaska HIGH LEVEL About 590 cases identified; most in populated areas. By Ann Potempa Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 15, 2001) Increasing flu cases in Alaska's most populated areas have led health officials to report a widespread outbreak to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. As of Thursday, Alaska was the only state in the nation reporting that level of flu activity. The state Section of Epidemiology has reported 578 cases of influenza type A and almost a dozen cases that were either type B or of unknown origin. "The magnitude of this has been much greater for this time period than last year," said Don Ritter, manager for the State Virology Laboratory in Fairbanks. Alaska's virology laboratory is one of a number of labs in the United S…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Manokotak man found drowned in Weary River ACCIDENT: One man lives after water claims snowmobile. By Anne Marie Tavella Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 8, 2002) A 40-year-old Manokotak man apparently drowned over the weekend while waiting to be rescued in the Weary River. A search-and-rescue team from Manokotak, 25 miles southwest of Dillingham, found John Toyukak's body about 2:30 p.m. Sunday submerged in 3 feet of water. Toyukak and Christian Tugatuk, 35, also of Manokotak, were returning to the village by snowmachine from Dillingham about 8 p.m. Saturday in darkness and blowing snow when the machine fell into a small section of open water, Tugatuk said. Tugatuk waded through the waist-deep water to the bank a short distance away and called Toyukak to follow him, he said. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Feeding the troops Army field cooks compete for the coveted Connelly Awards By T.C. Mitchell Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 8, 2002) Master Sgt. David Castle reviews recipes with Chief Warrant Officer Roger DeWitz while the 207th kitchen crew prepares a meal. DeWitz is a judge of the national Army cooks competition. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Thursday morning the place was just trees and snow. Saturday evening it was trees, snow, mobile kitchen, sanitation station, ration trucks, fuel depot, communications site, water trailer, an excited sergeant, a confident sergeant, a sergeant who takes no crap from the other sergeants, a Spec 1 who has no idea which sergeant to listen to and 60 cold, hungry soldiers. It worked out fine. In the backcountry bowels of Fo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Slain woman's neighbors knew she didn't trust son HOMICIDE: She believed he was stealing from her, friends say. By Tony Hopfinger Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 7, 2002) The body of Charlotte Miles was found at this home on Muldoon Road. Anchorage police have charged Miles' son Kenneth Padgett with her death. Padgett pleaded not guilty Saturday to two counts of murder. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Charlotte Miles, whose visiting son is accused of asphyxiating her in November and hiding her body inside the breakfast bar of her Muldoon mobile home, was so worried he would steal from her that she asked a neighbor to watch her purse. Neighbors at Rangeview Mobile Home Park said Miles feared her 37-year-old son, Kenneth Padgett of Texas, because she believed he …

summary: Nov. 6. Michael A. McConnell & Laura J. Watne Seung H. Oh & Dede Cronin Toby S. Gratton & Molly D. Beattie James O. Byas & Stacy N. Lyman Jeffrey V. Herring & Jennifer C. Martin Michael R. Sheveleva Damon R. Bradshaw & April D. Loyd Gregory S. Ko…

title: Easy rescue highlights hiking's dangers
summary: Near-perfect search-and-rescue conditions greeted emergency personnel assembled in Chugach State Park early last Sunday morning. The moon lit the slopes below Wolverine Peak almost as bright as day. The winds that often howl across the Front Range were still. The temperature hovered around freezing, mild by winter standards. And still Chief Ranger Jerry Lewanski worried that snowshoer Julie Upjohn would go hypothermic before she got off the mountain. Lured by clear skies and the almost unbelievably bright moon, Upjohn had set off on a late-night excursion to the 4,455-foot summit of Wolverine. "She decided she was going to do a night run up Wolverine," Lewanski said. Anchorage residents oriented toward the city might find this a little odd, but the people who regularly venture into the hal…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Fourteen racers taking an ultra racing challenge 267 MILES: Participants will travel by wheelchair or handcycle. By S. Jane Szabo Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 20, 2001) Fourteen racers will race in wheelchairs or handcycles from Fairbanks to Anchorage in the 16th annual Sadler's Midnight Sun Ultra Challenge starting Saturday. It's the longest wheelchair and handcycle race in the world, organized by Challenge Alaska. This year's field of participants includes three Alaskans: Larry Coutermarsh of Fairbanks, back for his 15th race; Mark Hufford of Eagle River, a UAA student; and Kevin Jackson, a civil engineer and member of the Challenge Alaska board of directors. Accidents brought all three to their support of Challenge Alaska, a nonprofit agency focusing on adaptive sports, t…

title: Woman traces Aleut-Unangan heritage  --  across her face
summary: Unalaska -- In a world where ancient traditions and cultures are disappearing, 20-year-old Laresa Syverson is proud of her Aleut-Unangan heritage. You can see that pride on her face. Syverson had her face tattooed in the ancient tradition of the Unangan. "I remember seeing an old woman with her face done when I was about 13," Syverson said. "I thought it was the most beautiful thing I'd ever seen." A soft indigo tattoo crosses her face, one-quarter of an inch wide, a pair of parallel lines with a broken centerline, the ancient pattern of the village of Unalaska. "People are trying to bring back many of our traditions, like weaving and bentwood visors." she said. "I thought this was a much more personal way, a beautiful personal adornment, a beautiful representation of the people." Syverson…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community datebook (Published: January 6, 2002) SCHOOL LUNCH MONDAY Anchorage: Elementary -- Pepperoni pizza or Belgian waffle with sausage links or chicken burrito, frozen juice cup, apple, milk. Middle school -- Domino's Pizza with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, milk. Mat-Su: Pepperoni pizza, cinnamon applesauce cup, mini box of raisins, milk (white or strawberry). TUESDAY Anchorage: Elementary -- Tacos or nachos or corn dog, pineapple bites and cheese, fruit leather, milk. Middle school -- Chicken Drummettes with additional entrees offered, fruit and vegetable, bread, milk. Mat-Su: Corn dog, Tater Bar, fresh fruit, animal crackers, milk (white or chocolate). TODAY COMMUNITY Americans United for Separation of Church and State, Alaska Chapter, public informa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Aleutian goose is en route to Russia CONNIE: Alaska bird shipped to Far East to help re-establish flocks. By Nancy Erickson Seward Phoenix Log (Published: September 4, 2001) Seward -- The Aleutian Canada goose hatched at the Alaska SeaLife Center last month has found a new home and a purpose in life. Connie, short for St. Constantine, an area of the Aleutian Islands where the egg was found, joined 17 others of his breed from Buldir Island as part of a captive breeding flock in a Russian wildlife facility earlier this month. Because Connie was hatched and raised in captivity, the flight on Magadan Air from Anchorage to Russia was quite uneventful for him compared with how it was for his counterparts, said Vernon Byrd, a wildlife biologist for Alaska Maritime National Wildlife Refuge. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | More entries to park eyed CHUGACH Superintendent wants space reserved for access points. By Tim Pryor Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 25, 2001) The head of Chugach State Park wants Anchorage to reserve space for more trail heads and parking lots before private land development on the Hillside makes that impossible. The city and state have missed some chances to link Anchorage's neighborhoods to the park by allowing new homes to block access, said park Superintendent Al Meiners. He wants to make sure the city preserves public access routes when land near the park is subdivided. "We've been cut off from park access from other parts of the park in the past," he said. "We'd like to see that not happen in the future." The city, while willing to help, wants more specifics. And nei…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Panel addresses 'digital divide' INTERNET: Solutions sought to bring reliable and affordable service to state's villages. By Tony Hopfinger Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 18, 2001) Rural Alaska saw an exodus between 1980 and 1990, with nearly 5,000 people trading their villages for urban cities. That's equal to a town the size of McGrath "dying every year," said Tom Harris, president of Alaska Village Initiatives. "If these people are going to stay, they need access to the market." They need the Internet, he said. Harris was among more than 30 Internet panelists Wednesday who participated in a rural communication forum at the Egan Center in downtown Anchorage. The conference was part of the Information Technology Expo that ended Wednesday. The idea was to bring together a m…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | FAA remembers workers at ceremony GARVEY: FAA administrator honors employees at memorial, then meets with airport officials. By Maureen Clark The Associated Press (Published: December 14, 2001) Federal Aviation Administrator Jane Garvey traveled to Anchorage for a memorial service Thursday to honor two FAA workers killed in the line of duty. Joyce Tucker of Anchorage and Ronald Frizzell of Wasilla were killed Oct. 18 when the helicopter they were riding in crashed into Cook Inlet. The two had performed work on navigation and communication aids on Fire Island and were returning to Anchorage. Era Aviation pilot Bob Larson also was killed. Two other FAA workers survived. "In a very real sense, they understood what public service is all about," Garvey told about 200 FAA workers and famil…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Bid to appeal Katie John denied RULING Superior Court judge rejects suit seeking injunction against Knowles. By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 26, 2001) A Superior Court judge said Tuesday he could not order Gov. Tony Knowles to appeal the Katie John case. "It's not my place as a judge to tell anyone, including the governor, to file an appeal," Judge John Reese ruled in a lawsuit asking that Knowles be ordered to appeal the case. "The governor's decision not to appeal Katie John was well within his discretion." The plaintiffs in the suit, represented by the Alaska Constitutional Legal Defense Conservation Fund, wanted an injunction ordering Knowles to file an appeal of the subsistence fishing case by Oct. 4, to meet a Supreme Court deadline. In rejecting…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Kenai erosion buffer hits snag SLOW DOWN: Critics say permit process is going too fast. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 21, 2001) Kenai -- The city of Kenai is pushing hard for a project that aims to stop nature in its tracks. It wants to end erosion of the sandy Kenai River bluff by lining its last mile with boulders. The boulders would be topped with a paved, Anchorage-style coastal trail. In the end, the proposed $10 million project would prevent wind and waves from chewing off chunks of city streets and buildings while adding an attractive footpath along one of the state's most productive salmon streams. It would be a nice place to walk and an attractive bonus if the city is to accomplish its long-term goal of building a hotel or convention center overlooki…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Orphans' haunted eyes draw Rotarians to Russia ORPHANAGE Volunteers work with abandoned, abused children in Siberia. By Melissa Campbell Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 26, 2001) Although he still can't explain why, Dave Sadlowski felt compelled to help children in Russia. It wasn't long into this new year when Sadlowski attended a presentation at the Anchorage East Rotary Club showing the plight of nearly 100 children living in an orphanage in the Russian town of Tomsk. Presenter Carolyn Jones, a regular volunteer at the orphanage, was looking to build the first-ever team of Rotary volunteers to put their lives on hold for up to a month to travel to the middle of Russia to work with these children. The trip was planned for the month of August. Jones showed Rotary members …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | North Slope gas opened to bidders ROYALTY: State hopes to spark interest and test the market. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 27, 2001) In a bid to stimulate exploration and test the market, the state is offering to sell North Slope natural gas directly to buyers for the first time. Bids can be made through the end of next month through the Alaska Department of Natural Resources. As owner of the land, the state collects a percentage of oil and gas produced from state leases. Typically the state collects cash from the producers instead of actually finding buyers for the gas. State officials also sometimes take this "royalty" oil or gas and sell it directly to the highest bidder. On the North Slope, the state has never taken that step before with its gas. But …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Haste to draw conclusions on gas pipeline is bad policy David Reaume (Published: December 16, 2001) I wish that I could understand why certain responsible public figures are so willing to make rash judgments in public about the proposed natural gas pipeline projects before all of the facts are on the table. For example, former gubernatorial candidate Jim Sykes, in a Nov. 19 Daily News opinion piece, asserted the following: "If any North Slope gas project is built, the needs of Alaskans must be met with a pipeline spur to the Railbelt." How does he come to the conclusion that a pipeline spur "must" be built? Where are his facts? Are we to assume that a pipeline spur should be built regardless of the cost? Should an otherwise viable gas line project to the Lower 48 be abandoned if a pi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Initiative backers sue over ballot language BIASED': Kalenka says group isn't trying to hide costs of moving session. By Martha Bellisle Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 24, 2001) Juneau -- The group behind an initiative to move legislative sessions to the Matanuska-Susitna Borough is suing the state, claiming that the wording of the measure it hopes to get on next year's ballot is "biased" and "misleading." "It makes it look like our group is trying to hide the costs of the move from voters," Uwe Kalenka, president of Alaskans for Efficient Government, said Thursday in a telephone interview. The lawsuit, filed Monday in Anchorage Superior Court, says that when Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer wrote the summary of the measure, she used language proposed by people who are against moving the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Volunteer Opportunities (Published: December 27, 2001) The Volunteer Center is a community resource center sponsored by United Way of Anchorage. To volunteer, call listings below or contact Sue Brogan at the Volunteer Center at 263-3821 or sbrogan@ak.org . For information online, see www.uway.ak.org . Volunteers are needed in these agencies: Girl Scouts Susitna Council: Women in science- and technology-related fields are needed to lead workshops for girls at Women of Science & Technology Day, Feb. 2, in Anchorage and Wasilla. (Terry, 248-0805, Ext. 316, Anchorage; Mary, 376-3822, Wasilla) Nine Star Education Center: Adult tutors needed for adult basic education and English literacy programs. Training provided. (Dawn, 279-7827 or dawnb@ninestar.com ) Library Resources, Anchorage Schoo…

title: Avalanche cuts loose on Flattop
summary: An avalanche engulfed the west side of Flattop Mountain earlier this week, Chugach State Park officials said. Fortunately, it let go when no one was on the popular snowboarding slope. Chief Park Ranger Jerry Lewanski said he believed the slide occurred late Sunday night or early Monday morning. Death Gully, on the northwest face of Flattop, the part most visible from Anchorage, was covered in the slide. Natural releases are common in the area, particularly after storms. What is significant about this slide is that it happened in an area often frequented by people, Lewanski said. He said he is not aware of anyone being injured in the avalanche. The area is known for dangerous slides. "Luckily, it came down during the night," he said. "The area around Flattop has a history of hurting and kil…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Seeing whales Calf sightings encourage observers of Inlet belugas By Doug O'harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 23, 2001) Peter Merryman, Tyonek village president, and NMFS biologist Barbara Mahoney put satellite transmitters on the beluga. (Photo by Bill Gossweiler) Dan Vos (left), Matt Eagleton and Brad Smith (far right, in dry suit), all NMFS biologists, work with one of the whales. (Photo by Bill Gossweiler) As they roamed Cook Inlet in pursuit of salmon this summer, depleted beluga whales nursed a new generation of youngsters and spread into fresh seas, offering evidence that a decade-long decline may be ending, according to a Tyonek whaling captain and federal biologists. "I think they're holding steady," said Peter Merryman, chairman of the Cook Inlet Marine Mamma…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Federal funds ease classroom crowding GOALS: Fewer students per teacher means better learning opportunities. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 23, 2001) Anchorage public schools, especially in the lower grades, have smaller classes on average this year, Superintendent Carol Comeau said in her annual report to the School Board Monday night. Grades two, three, four and five and the core middle school subjects average about one less student per class than last year. High school classes with fewer than 20 students increased, generally, and those with more than 35 students decreased. Smaller classes improve education, Comeau said, giving teachers more time with each student and making it easier to manage classrooms. A low teacher-to-students ratio is especially …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Alaskans want vote on rural priority SURVEY:Residents prefer to decide subsistence issue. By Martha Bellisle Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 16, 2001) Juneau -- Three of every four Alaskans polled this month said they want the chance to vote on a constitutional amendment guaranteeing a rural subsistence priority. Half said they would vote for such a measure. The statewide poll of 272 supervoters, commissioned by Cook Inlet Region Inc. and conducted by Ivan Moore Research, is the latest snapshot of how Alaskans feel about the subsistence debate. Supervoters are households in which at least one member voted in three of the past four statewide elections. The survey had a 6 percent margin of error, Moore said Wednesday. It showed 76 percent favor putting an amendment on the ba…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Murkowski sees dream slip away ANWR: Drilling seemed so close, but it was just beyond his grasp. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 27, 2001) Washington -- For Sen. Frank Murkowski, 2001 started with such promise. Republicans controlled the White House and both houses of Congress for the first time in 45 years, and energy policy was high on their agenda. Murkowski himself was chairman of the Senate Energy Committee. Opening the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil drilling -- the biggest goal of his 21 year Senate career -- never seemed closer. But he and the other two members of Alaska's congressional delegation are returning to Alaska at year's end without the ANWR legislation they seek. What happened? Murkowski, the Senate's prime champion of ANWR drilling, …

title: Soldotna hospital considers expanding
summary: Kenai -- An expansion plan for Central Peninsula General Hospital in Soldotna could nearly double its square footage, put a satellite facility in Kenai and cost up to $70 million. Hospital board president Diana Zirul said last week that expansion plans are emerging from the hospital's two-year strategic planning process. "There are many systemic and structural issues with the hospital," Zirul said. "We have a number of space needs." She said the ceilings in the oldest part of the hospital are too low for today's medical technology and the layout could be improved. The hospital opened 31 years ago and has been expanded twice. The hospital originally was designed to serve 5,000. It now serves about 34,000. A preliminary plan calls for an expansion that could displace some homes along the per…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Missile radar may not go on Shemya TEST BED: Group alleges military has altered its defense plan. The Associated Press (Published: September 2, 2001) Fairbanks -- An environmental group that filed a lawsuit against the Pentagon over a new missile defense plan alleges the military has dropped all thought of building a powerful radar on Shemya Island in the Aleutians. A missile office spokeswoman, however, said the location remains an option. In its lawsuit, the Natural Resources Defense Council alleges that the military's new "test bed" proposes placing the powerful detection device on Hawaii or even in an airplane. "I think it's pretty clear they aren't going to put it on Shemya," Christopher Paine, NRDC's attorney on the case, told the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner. He said he obtained…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: January 5, 2002) Local bank reports record earnings First National Bank of Alaska reported record profits last year of $38.8 million, up 24 percent over 2000, said D.H. Cuddy, chairman and president of the Anchorage-based bank. The previous record earnings were $35 million in 1993. Fourth-quarter profits grew 19 percent to $10.9 million, he said. As of Dec. 31, the bank's loan portfolio reached $915 million, up 19 percent from a year earlier. Total assets, including loans, were $1.79 billion, up 7 percent. Deposits grew 8 percent to $1.1 billion, Cuddy said. Real estate brokers merge Two Anchorage commercial real estate brokerage, management and leasing companies have merged. Pacific Tower Properties Inc. and H&R Management Inc. will operate under t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: January 4, 2002) Refrigerant spills at pipeline site Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. estimated Wednesday that nearly 25,000 gallons of refrigerant brine solution may have spilled from the refrigeration loop beneath the warehouse and shop area at a North Slope pipeline pump station. Crews were working to pinpoint the location of a leak that caused the spill at Pump Station No. 1 of the trans-Alaska oil pipeline. The refrigeration line keeps the gravel pad under the warehouse frozen to maintain its structural integrity. The brine is a mix of water, calcium chloride and hexavalent chromium. Alyeska officials said they were not sure when the spill occurred. It was discovered and reported Tuesday. Workers plan to use a fiber-optic camera to examine the insi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Nome youth home director ousted CONFRONTATION: Clients moved while state investigates. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 4, 2002) The acting director of a Nome youth home was dismissed Wednesday after a week on the job and five days after the state removed the home's teenage residents. The Nome Receiving Home's board of directors unanimously decided to terminate Theresa Rismeyer days after she announced a series of new policies that rankled staffers, according to Norma Niclas, board president. "Theresa had some very good ideas," Niclas said. "But it was like too much, too soon." On Dec. 28, about eight disgruntled staffers angrily confronted Rismeyer in a scene witnessed by at least two teens from the home. Rismeyer described the confrontation as involving scr…

title: FCC grants rural Web exception
summary: Rural Alaskans may tap into their schools' Internet access that sits idle when school isn't in session, the Federal Communications Commission announced Monday. The decision, which applies only to Alaska, could make the Internet far more affordable outside of urban areas, said Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer. The decision will bring parity between urban and rural rates for online service, said Ulmer, who serves on state and federal information technology committees and helped lobby for the change. "There were people with phone bills in excess of $500 per month in our district," said Karl Kowalski, director of instructional technology for the Northwest Arctic Borough School District in Kotzebue. On top of exorbitant prices, he said, the service was poor. As many as 240 communities could benefit, accordi…

title: Ex-senator Mackie to lobby for Juneau, focus on keeping capital
summary: Former state Sen. Jerry Mackie has been hired by the city of Juneau to a one-year, $40,000 contract as a lobbyist. Mackie, who served both as a Democrat and Republican before leaving the Senate in 2000, will focus on gaining support for keeping the capital in Juneau, said Mayor Sally Smith. The Juneau Assembly approved the contract Monday. Mackie served in the state House from 1990 to 1996 and retired as Senate majority leader. He began working as a government relations manager for Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. after leaving state government. He will leave that post to become an independent lobbyist, Mackie said. Mackie will represent Alyeska, Cook Inlet Region Inc., Holland America and Arctic Slope Regional Corp. subsidiaries Natchiq Inc. and Petro Star Inc. State ethics codes prohibit for…

title: Coming Sunday in Outdoors
summary: Biologist Vic Van Ballenberghe spent four years observing a record-size bull moose in Denali National Park. "His antlers not only had a wide spread but an exceptional mass, too. The palms were long and wide with distinct tines. The bases of the antlers were simply massive. And the left antler was abnormal, with a few points rising strangely from the center of the palm. His body matched his headgear. At about 1,600 pounds, he was as big as a moose gets," Van Ballenberghe writes. Alcohol -- the new, old fuel that revolutionized Iditarod Trail cooking -- is now available in an efficient stove for skiers, snowshoers and backpackers . No more fiddling with preheating white gas stoves or cleaning clogged kerosene burners. The Hike N' Light is a stove so light and simple it looks, well, almost ri…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Net dividend process improved APPLICATIONS: State's latest online permanent fund system is quick and easy. By The Associated Press (Published: January 2, 2002) Alaskans can begin applying for the 2002 Alaska Permanent Fund today, but state officials hope they choose an improved online application available Jan. 7. "The new system is similar to buying a book or an airline ticket on the Internet," said Nanci Jones, director of the Permanent Fund Dividend Division at the Department of Revenue. "There is no cumbersome software to download as last year. It's just a matter of clicking through the screens, filling in the information, and checking the boxes." Online applicants also need valid Social Security numbers, must be U.S. citizens, and must have a printer connected to their computer.…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: SUNDAY COMMUNITY The Alaska Native Heritage Center hosts Family Fun Weekend with artist Amanda Attla and the Tlingit & Haida Dancers of Anchorage, noon-5 p.m., at the center, off North Muldoon Road -- free with regular admission: $8 adults, $5 youths 7-17, free younger than 6. (330-8000) Anchorage Widowed Persons Support group, for newly widowed persons, 1:30 p.m. Sunday, Anchorage Senior Center, 1300 E 19th Ave. Men and women widowed for longer periods of time are also welcome. (566-0580) Blood Bank of Alaska holiday blood drive, 10 a.m.-7 p.m., second floor, Dimond Center, across from Dimond Center Cinemas. Give the gift of life this holiday season. All donors receive free gift wrapping and one free video rental coupon from Blockbuster Video. (563-3110) Eagle River Nature Center holds a …

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Brighten the holidays for area foster children Alyeska Resort and the Division of Family and Youth Services have teamed up for the third annual Giving Tree program to provide gifts for foster children. To participate, visit the resort's booth behind the Santa display in The Mall at Sears, choose the name of a child from the list, buy a gift and return it to the booth before Dec. 22. Or buy a gift certificate and a program volunteer will do the shopping. Those who buy a gift can hang an ornament on the Christmas tree. For information, call Cella Baker, 754-2209. Car dealer giving away car for the holidays Phil Haws Auto Outlet is offering one free car to someone in the community. To apply, contact the auto dealer by Thursday at Phil Haws Auto Outlet, 10100 Old Seward Highway, Anchorage 9951…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Rape admission went unreported UAF: Campus police later arrested suspect for subsequent assaults. The Associated Press (Published: January 1, 2002) Fairbanks -- Police at the University of Alaska Fairbanks are disturbed that the court system didn't tell them about a university basketball player's rape admission shortly before he was accused of kidnapping the same woman. Cameron Watts, 18, admitted the assault on his former girlfriend in a hearing on a domestic violence restraining order. But court officials say they had no obligation to report what he said. At the Nov. 21 hearing, Watts' 18-year-old former girlfriend said he tied her up and raped her in her dorm room on Nov. 2. Watts, at the hearing, said, "Her story is correct." A magistrate granted the woman a six-month restraining…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Kenai fishing plan surfaces COALITION Goal is to give Peninsula residents first crack at some salmon runs. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 30, 2001) A coalition of people with deep roots on the Kenai Peninsula says it has come up with a blueprint for solving divisive fishing disputes, but not everyone is buying into the plan. The group, the Kenai Peninsula Resource Management Coalition, was formed two years ago but only recently has made a ripple, by approaching the recent federal subsistence board and unveiling a mission statement that aims to rewrite the way salmon fishing is conducted in Cook Inlet. The group says it plans to take the idea to the state Board of Fisheries at its next meeting in February. The overarching goal is to give Kenai Peninsula reside…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | In Brief (Published: December 31, 2001) ANCHORAGE Weather strands Aleutian-bound passengers High winds and poor visibility in Dutch Harbor have stranded about 400 people in Anchorage because flights out to the Aleutian Islands community have been canceled for days. Alaska Airlines' two scheduled flights to Dutch Harbor were canceled again Sunday. Spokesman Jack Walsh said he believes the last flight that made it out there was Wednesday. A special Sunday afternoon flight did take off after the weather improved, Walsh said, and was near Cold Bay a little after 4:30 p.m. The ceiling dropped to about 700 feet, however, and it appeared the flight, with 43 passengers aboard, would have to return to Anchorage. As of Saturday, 350 Dutch Harbor passengers were stranded, Walsh said. He didn't …
